ai_enrichment.summary: Abimelech é um nome comum entre os reis filisteus, semelhante a 'Faraó' no Egito. O primeiro Abimelech mencionado na Bíblia é o rei de Gerar durante a época de Abraão, que, após um incidente envolvendo Sara, fez um pacto de paz com Abraão. O segundo Abimelech, possivelmente seu filho, também foi rei de Gerar durante a época de Isaque, que buscou refúgio em sua terra durante uma fome. A história de Abimelech ilustra a interação entre os patriarcas hebreus e os povos vizinhos, destacando temas de providência divina e alianças.
outline: 1. Abimelech, rei de Gerar durante a época de Abraão (Gênesis 20:1-18): O encontro entre Abimelech e Abraão, onde Sara é restaurada a Abraão após um incidente de engano.
2. O pacto de paz entre Abimelech e Abraão (Gênesis 21:22-34): A aliança estabelecida em Beer-sheba, simbolizando a relação pacífica entre os hebreus e os filisteus.

3. Abimelech, rei de Gerar durante a época de Isaque (Gênesis 26:1-22): Isaque busca abrigo em Gerar e repete o engano de seu pai, refletindo a continuidade das interações entre os patriarcas e os filisteus.
4. A importância do nome Abimelech na cultura filisteia: Análise do nome como um título comum entre os reis filisteus, semelhante a 'Faraó' entre os egípcios.
ai_enrichment.theological_significance: A figura de Abimelech destaca a providência de Deus na proteção dos patriarcas e a importância das alianças na narrativa bíblica. As interações entre os hebreus e os filisteus revelam como Deus opera através de circunstâncias complexas para cumprir Seus propósitos, além de ilustrar a necessidade de integridade e verdade nas relações humanas.

ai_themes_normalized.label_original: Providência divina na proteção dos patriarcas
ai_themes_normalized.aspect: A intervenção de Deus na vida de Abraão e Isaque
A importância das alianças na narrativa bíblica
As lições sobre a verdade nas interações humanas

definitions.text: My father a king, or father of a king, a common name of the Philistine kings, as “Pharaoh” was of the Egyptian kings. (1.) The Philistine king of Gerar in the time of Abraham (Gen. 20:1-18). By an interposition of Providence, Sarah was delivered from his harem, and was restored to her husband Abraham. As a mark of respect he gave to Abraham valuable gifts, and offered him a settlement in any part of his country; while at the same time he delicately and yet severely rebuked him for having practised a deception upon him in pretending that Sarah was only his sister. Among the gifts presented by the king were a thousand pieces of silver as a “covering of the eyes” for Sarah; i.e., either as an atoning gift and a testimony of her innocence in the sight of all, or rather for the purpose of procuring a veil for Sarah to conceal her beauty, and thus as a reproof to her for not having worn a veil which, as a married woman, she ought to have done. A few years after this Abimelech visited Abraham, who had removed southward beyond his territory, and there entered into a league of peace and friendship with him. This league was the first of which we have any record. It was confirmed by a mutual oath at Beer-sheba (Gen. 21:22-34). (2.) A king of Gerar in the time of Isaac, probably the son of the preceeding (Gen. 26:1-22). Isaac sought refuge in his territory during a famine, and there he acted a part with reference to his wife Rebekah similar to that of his father Abraham with reference to Sarah. Abimelech rebuked him for the deception, which he accidentally discovered. Isaac settled for a while here, and prospered. Abimelech desired him, however, to leave his territory, which Isaac did. Abimelech afterwards visited him when he was encamped at Beer-sheba, and expressed a desire to renew the covenant which had been entered into between their fathers (Gen. 26:26-31). (3.) A son of Gideon (Judg. 9:1), who was proclaimed king after the death of his father (Judg. 8:33-9:6). One of his first acts was to murder his brothers, seventy in number, “on one stone,” at Ophrah. Only one named Jotham escaped. He was an unprincipled, ambitious ruler, often engaged in war with his own subjects. When engaged in reducing the town of Thebez, which had revolted, he was struck mortally on his head by a mill-stone, thrown by the hand of a woman from the wall above. Perceiving that the wound was mortal, he desired his armour-bearer to thrust him through with his sword, that it might not be said he had perished by the hand of a woman (Judg. 9:50-57). (4.) The son of Abiathar, and high priest in the time of David (1 Chr. 18:16). In the parallel passage, 2 Sam. 8:17, we have the name Ahimelech, and Abiathar, the son of Ahimelech. This most authorities consider the more correct reading. (5.) Achish, king of Gath, in the title of Ps. 34. (Comp. 1 Sam. 21:10-15.)

(father of the king), the name of several Philistine kings, was probably a common title of these kings, like that of Pharaoh among the Egyptians and that of Caesar and Augustus among the Romans. Hence in the title of (Psalms 34:1) ... the name of Abimelech is given to the king, who is called Achish in (1 Samuel 21:11)

ai_entities.name: Abimelech
ai_entities.context: Rei filisteu de Gerar durante a época de Abraão e Isaque

ai_entities.name: Isaque
ai_entities.context: Filho de Abraão, que também interagiu com Abimelech

ai_discovered_aspects.label: Cultural Interactions
ai_discovered_aspects.rationale: As interações entre Abimelech e os patriarcas refletem a complexidade das relações culturais e políticas na antiga Canaã.

ai_discovered_aspects.label: Moral Lessons
ai_discovered_aspects.rationale: As histórias de engano de Abraão e Isaque ensinam lições morais sobre a importância da honestidade e da confiança em Deus.

ai_relationships.description: Abimelech viveu na mesma época que Abraão e teve interações significativas com ele.
Abimelech também interagiu com Isaque, filho de Abraão, em um contexto semelhante.
